title: Painting kitchen cupboard doors and carcasses
subtitle: Strip, sand, prime, and repaint kitchen units using specialist furniture paint for a durable factory-smooth finish
excerpt: How to repaint kitchen cupboard doors and carcasses in specialist furniture paint. Covers degreasing, sanding, priming bare or previously painted surfaces, and applying two topcoats for a hard, washable finish.
sourceNotes: Black and Decker Complete Photo Guide to Home Improvement (referenced, not reproduced). Collins Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced). UK trade practice for kitchen-painting contracts.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hard-setting paint formulated for furniture and joinery. Water-based alkyd paints dry faster and have lower odour than traditional oil-based gloss; they cure to a harder, more washable surface than standard emulsion. Used on kitchen units, skirting boards, and painted furniture. Marketed under names such as Farrow and Ball Estate Eggshell, Zinsser AllCoat, and Little Greene Intelligent Eggshell.

content.text: Painting kitchen cupboard doors and carcasses gives a kitchen a near-complete transformation without replacement. The approach is degreasing, sanding, priming, and two topcoats of
. The job is methodical rather than difficult; the prep is where the quality lives.

Remove all doors. Label each door and its hinge positions with masking tape and a pencil so they go back correctly. Remove all handles, knobs, and hinge covers. Set hinges aside in a labelled bag. Work on the doors flat; painting vertically causes sags in thick furniture paint.

Kitchen surfaces carry years of cooking grease and cleaning-product residue. Wipe every surface (doors, carcass faces, drawer fronts) with a proprietary kitchen degreaser or a 1:10 solution of sugar soap and water. Rinse with clean water and allow to dry completely before sanding. A surface that feels slightly tacky after degreasing still has grease on it; degrease again.

Sand all surfaces with 120-grit to break the existing finish and give the primer a mechanical key. Work with the grain on any timber or MDF panel. Vacuum off the dust, then wipe with a damp cloth. Fill any chips, dents, or screw holes with a fine surface filler. Sand back the filled areas to flush when dry, finishing at 180-grit. Any raised grain on MDF edges needs extra attention (sand until the edge feels as smooth as the face.

Apply one coat of a bonding primer or a dedicated kitchen-cabinet primer. Use a mini roller for flat faces and a 25 mm brush for recesses, rails, and stiles. Allow to dry fully, then cut back lightly with 240-grit before the topcoat. Priming is not optional) it seals porous MDF edges, blocks tannin bleed from oak, and gives the topcoat something to grip.

Apply the first topcoat with a mini roller on flat faces; tip off immediately with a dry 50 mm brush in long strokes along the grain to eliminate roller stipple. Do recesses and detail with the 25 mm brush. Allow to dry fully between coats (4 hours minimum for water-based alkyd, overnight for oil-based. Cut back lightly with 320-grit between coats. Apply the second topcoat in the same way. Two coats over a primed surface give a durable, washable finish. A third coat is worthwhile if the coverage looks thin after the second.

Allow the final coat 24 hours minimum before rehanging. The paint is touch-dry long before it is fully cured; hanging too early and closing the doors will press the faces together and transfer paint. Replace doors on the labelled hinges. Refit handles. Adjust hinge screws to correct any alignment problems that were present before the repaint.

attrs.symptom: Paint peels or flakes off in sheets within a few weeks
attrs.cause: The surface was not degreased adequately before priming, or primer was skipped entirely.
attrs.fix: Strip back to bare or the last sound coat, degrease twice, prime, then reapply topcoats.

attrs.symptom: Brush marks visible in the dried topcoat
attrs.cause: The paint was applied too thickly or the brush tip-off was too slow) the surface skinned before the tip-off stroke could level it.
attrs.fix: Sand back with 320-grit and apply a thinner coat, tipping off immediately after rolling while the surface is still wet.
